nativeRange.habitat: In the wild, this fern occupies subtropical to tropical riparian zones — streams, riverbanks, and shaded forest margins — where it typically grows attached to wet rocks or wood at the water's edge, often only partially submerged rather than fully aquatic.

summary: The Asian Water Fern is a compact epiphytic fern from tropical and subtropical Asia, typically reaching 5–15 cm when submerged. It attaches to driftwood or rock rather than rooting into substrate, and is generally considered an undemanding plant suited to low-light aquariums.
careNotes:
The Asian Water Fern (Bolbitis heteroclita) is a small epiphytic rhizome fern from subtropical and tropical Asia and out into Melanesia and Micronesia, where it grows along stream margins and riverbanks, typically attached to wet rocks or wood rather than submerged in open water. It is noticeably smaller than its African relative Bolbitis heudelotii (see [fw-plant-009]): submerged specimens generally reach about 5–15 cm rather than the 15–40 cm of that species, and the fronds have a more undivided, Java-fern-like appearance rather than the heavily divided form of B. heudelotii. A small-leafed cultivar from the Philippines — sometimes sold separately as 'Bolbitis Difformis' or 'El Nino Fern' — stays even more compact.

Like all Bolbitis, this fern must be attached to hardscape rather than planted into substrate. Tie or glue the rhizome (the thick horizontal stem from which the fronds grow) to driftwood or porous rock using cotton thread, fishing line, plant-safe mesh, or a small amount of cyanoacrylate glue, and allow the roots to anchor naturally over time. NEVER bury the rhizome in substrate — covering it reliably causes rot.

Low light is generally recommended; low-to-moderate light can also work, though intense direct LED may scorch fronds or encourage algae growth on the leaves. CO2 injection is not required but tends to noticeably accelerate what is otherwise a slow-growing plant. Water should be soft to moderately hard and slightly acidic to neutral; stability matters more than hitting an exact value, as sudden swings in pH or temperature have been reported to trigger leaf melt. Moderate flow helps the plant shed debris. Plants grown out of water (emersed) may shed their initial fronds when first introduced to an aquarium and take some time before producing fully aquatic-form leaves.
breedingNotes: Propagated vegetatively by cutting the horizontal rhizome into segments, each retaining at least one frond and several roots, and attaching each segment to a fresh piece of hardscape. Adventitious plantlets also develop on mature fronds in some specimens and can be detached once they carry their own small rhizome and roots. The species reproduces sexually by spores in its emersed form, but submerged-only specimens rarely sporulate in aquaria.

primary.name: Buce Plant
primary.notes: Primary source for lighting (low), CO2 (not necessary), growth rate (slow), placement (epiphyte), pH (6.0–7.5), and rhizome-burial warning.

additional.name: Modern Aquarium
additional.notes: Cross-checked epiphyte placement, slow growth, low-to-moderate lighting, CO2-not-required, and rhizome-burial warning. Provided temperature range (72–82°F = 22.2–27.8°C, rounded to 22–28°C). Disagrees slightly with Buce on lighting (low vs low–moderate); kept low as the more common signal.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Taxonomy fallback (Dryopteridaceae, Polypodiales), native-range countries, synonym Bolbitis asiatica, and confirmation that 'Difformis' is a small-leafed cultivar from the Philippines.

additional.name: AquaTerra Obsession
additional.notes: Third source used (via web search summary) to confirm temperature 22–25°C (with broader 20–28°C reported), leaf length 5–10 cm, max aquarium height ~4–6 inches (10–15 cm), and parameter-stability sensitivity.
